questions.question: Which set of changes to a metal wire will definitely result in an increase in its resistance?
options: Increasing the length and decreasing the thickness
Decreasing the length and increasing the thickness
Increasing the length and increasing the thickness
Decreasing the length and decreasing the thickness
questions.explanation:
Resistance is proportional to length and inversely proportional to area (thickness). To increase $R$, you need a longer and thinner wire. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting Row C, thinking bigger wires always have higher resistance.

questions.question: The table shows the results of an experiment on a component. What can be concluded about the component?nn| $V / \text{V}$ | $1.0$ | $2.0$ | $3.0$ | $4.0$ |n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |n| $I / \text{mA}$ | $0.5$ | $1.1$ | $1.6$ | $2.1$ |
options: It is a non-ohmic conductor because the resistance is not constant.
It is an ohmic conductor because the current increases with voltage.
The resistance of the component is constant at $2000\text{ }Omega$.
The component is a diode.
questions.explanation:
Calculating $R = V/I$ for each pair: $1/0.0005 = 2000\text{ }Omega$, $2/0.0011 \approx 1818\text{ }Omega$, $3/0.0016 = 1875\text{ }Omega$. Since the resistance changes, it is non-ohmic. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking 'ohmic' just means the current goes up when voltage goes up.

questions.question: Which graph represents the $I-V$ characteristic of a semiconductor diode?
options: A graph with zero current for negative voltage and a sharp rise for positive voltage
A straight line passing through the origin
A curve whose gradient decreases as voltage increases
A horizontal line at a constant current value
questions.explanation:
As described in Table 16.1, a diode allows current to flow in one direction only, represented by zero current on one side of the axis and a rise on the other. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting the straight line, which represents a fixed resistor.

questions.question: The diagram shows a circuit with a $6.0\text{ V}$ battery and a $12\text{ }Omega$ resistor. What is the reading on the ammeter?nn[Diagram: Simple series circuit with battery, ammeter, and one resistor]
options: $72\text{ A}$
$18\text{ A}$
questions.explanation:
Using $I = V / R = 6.0 / 12 = 0.5\text{ A}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing^12 by^6 to get $2.0\text{ A}$.

questions.question: A student measures the resistance of a length of wire. He then doubles the length of the wire and doubles its diameter. How does the new resistance compare to the original?
options: It is half of the original.
It is double the original.
$It is four \times the original.$
It is the same as the original.
questions.explanation:
Doubling length ($L$) doubles $R$. Doubling diameter ($d$) quadruples the area ($A$), which quarters the $R$. Overall effect: $2 \times (1/4) = 0.5$ (half). 

**Common mistake:** Thinking doubling diameter also just doubles the area, leading to 'the same' resistance.
